plan: 1. Audit all CSS files for animation/transition properties that lack prefers-reduced-motion overrides. Files to check: button.css (has transition, covered by shell.css global rule but should have explicit override for clarity), tokens.css (defines transition tokens — consider adding reduced-motion token overrides).

2. Harden intro-sequence.css reduced-motion block: change `transition: opacity 100ms ease-out` to `transition: none` for both .intro-line and .intro-overlay so intro content appears instantly with zero transition delay when reduced motion is preferred.

3. Harden intro-sequence.ts reduced-motion path: when reducedMotion is true, show all three lines immediately (already done), but reduce the 800ms hold delay to ~0ms or a minimal 50ms, and skip the fade-out entirely — just remove the overlay synchronously. This ensures no perceptible animation occurs.

4. Ensure AC3 (no content hidden when motion disabled): verify that .intro-line elements start visible (opacity: 1, transform: none) under prefers-reduced-motion so there is never a frame where content is invisible. Add a CSS rule under the reduced-motion media query: `.intro-line { opacity: 1; transform: none; }` so content is visible even before JS adds the --visible class.
5. Add explicit prefers-reduced-motion override in button.css to set `transition: none` for .btn, removing reliance on the shell.css global catch-all for buttons that may render outside .shell.

6. Create tests/us-033/reduced-motion.test.ts with the following test cases:
   - AC1: Intro CSS contains prefers-reduced-motion media query that sets transition to none (not just reduced)
   - AC1: Intro JS path with reduced motion completes within 200ms (verifying minimal delay)
   - AC2: All CSS files with transitions have corresponding prefers-reduced-motion overrides (static file scan)
   - AC3: Under reduced motion, intro lines have opacity: 1 and transform: none by default (no content hidden)
   - AC3: All content elements remain visible when prefers-reduced-motion is active (no display:none or visibility:hidden conditioned on motion)
7. Run npm run lint && npm run typecheck && npm run test to verify all changes pass.
8. Verify the global shell.css catch-all rule still functions as a safety net for any transitions missed by component-level overrides.
testing_recommendations: Unit test: verify intro-sequence CSS has `transition: none` (not just reduced duration) under prefers-reduced-motion
Unit test: verify intro JS completes in under 200ms when reduced motion is active
Unit test: static scan of all CSS files confirming every file with `transition` or `animation` properties also contains a `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce` media query
Unit test: verify .intro-line default styles under reduced-motion include opacity: 1 and transform: none
Unit test: verify no CSS rule hides content (display:none, visibility:hidden) conditionally on motion preference
Integration test: render full intro with matchMedia mocked to reduced-motion=true, confirm all three text lines are immediately readable (opacity 1) in the DOM
Run existing test suites (npm run test) to ensure no regressions in intro-sequence, receipt, permission-debt modules
Manual verification: enable 'Reduce motion' in macOS System Settings > Accessibility > Display, load the app, confirm no animations play and all content is immediately visible
risks: Over-aggressive transition removal could make interactive feedback (button hover states) feel broken — ensure hover/focus visual changes still occur instantly via property changes rather than transitions

The global shell.css catch-all uses !important which could conflict with component-level reduced-motion rules — ensure component rules don't inadvertently re-enable transitions that the global rule suppresses

Intro overlay removal timing: if the overlay is removed too quickly under reduced motion, users may not have time to read the privacy statement — consider keeping a brief readable hold (500-800ms) even with no animation

The intro-line opacity:0 default combined with JS class addition creates a brief flash-of-invisible-content; the CSS-only fix (opacity:1 under reduced-motion) resolves this but must be tested across browsers
